growth_records.medium: lactate + sulfate medium (D. vulgaris grown on lactate and sulfate; values from Noguera et al 1998)

up.method: experimental overall average, Table 2 (from Noguera et al 1998)
experimental overall average, Table 2 (from Noguera et al 1998)

sec.method: experimental overall average, Table 2 (from Noguera et al 1998)
experimental overall average (timepoints 0.05, 0.002, 0.04, 0.03), Table 2 (from Noguera et al 1998)

growth_records.cite: Stolyar et al. Metabolic modeling of a mutualistic microbial community. Molecular Systems Biology (2007). DOI:10.1038/msb4100131. [PMC1847946]
growth_records.snippet: Table 2 (experimental first row, overall average): Lactate uptake 0.27, Sulfate uptake 0.13, Acetate production 0.35, Hydrogen production ~0.00 (mM/h); Biomass production 3.11 mg DCW/l h. 'published data of D. vulgaris grown on lactate and
growth_records.notes: PROVENANCE: the experimental D. vulgaris values in Table 2 are reproduced from Noguera et al 1998, NOT measured in this paper. Overall-average experimental rates: lactate uptake 0.27 mM/h, sulfate uptake 0.13 mM/h, acetate production 0.35 mM/h, H2 production ~0.00 mM/h (per-timepoint 0.05/0.002/0.04/0.03 at 8/12/16/24 h), biomass production 3.11 mg DCW/L/h. Volumetric rates (mM/h and mg DCW/L/h), not per gDW. Medium composition for this Noguera dataset is NOT stated in the present paper (routine D. vulgaris culturing here was B3m, pH 7.2, 37 C, 80% N2:20% CO2, 30 mM lactate, 1.4 g/L Na2SO4 — but that is not necessarily Noguera's medium), so medium_composition_stated=false. No mu/doubling derivable (standing biomass not given).

growth_records.medium: Defined lactate/sulfate chemostat medium (full composition in refs 11 and 36; not restated here)

up.method: reported: sulfate present in excess (28 mM influent), ~50% consumed; volumetric uptake rate not given
reported qualitatively: lactate was the limiting substrate, added in stoichiometric proportions to facilitate 50% sulfate consumption; no numeric uptake rate

sec.units: fmol H2S per cell per day
sec.method: reported: metabolic rates ranged 0.9 to 146.7 fmol H2S per cell per day across the range of growth (dilution) rates at 28 mM sulfate; aqueous sulfide <1 uM (swept out)

growth_records.cite: Bertran et al. Oxygen isotope effects during microbial sulfate reduction: applications to sediment cell abundances. The ISME Journal (2020). DOI:10.1038/s41396-020-0618-2. [PMC7242377]
growth_records.snippet: Measured metabolic rates ranged from 0.9 to 146.7 fmol H2S per cell per day when sulfate concentrations were held at 28 mM ... In all experiments, lactate was the limiting substrate and added in stoichiometric proportions to facilitate 50%
growth_records.notes: Pure-culture chemostat across a range of growth rates. Growth physiology reported as cell-specific rates (fmol H2S per cell per day), NOT per gDW and NOT as mu in h-1 (dilution rates not tabulated in main text). csSRR = sulfide (H2S) production per cell = sulfate reduction per cell; sulfide kept <1 uM by continuous gas sweep, so it is effectively a secretion flux. Full medium composition given in cited refs 11 and 36, not in this article (medium_composition_stated=false); only sulfate (28 mM influent, ~50% consumed) and lactate (limiting) stated. Lactate uptake not fabricated (stoichiometric coefficient not given). 34epsilon range is an isotope metric, retained in yields for context, not a flux.

growth_records.strain: D. vulgaris Hildenborough + M. maripaludis S2
growth_records.medium: Coculture medium (CCM), no sulfate

up.units: mM consumed (steady-state; not a specific flux)
up.method: Feed 30 mM lactate minus steady-state residual 4 mM lactate = 26 mM consumed by D. vulgaris; cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h because no biomass dry weight is reported
Interspecies electron carrier consumed by M. maripaludis; reported steady-state headspace partial pressure, not a specific flux
Substrate for hydrogenotrophic methanogenesis; reported steady-state headspace partial pressure

sec.method: Product of D. vulgaris lactate fermentation; reported steady-state concentration, not a specific flux
Product of M. maripaludis methanogenesis; reported steady-state headspace partial pressure

growth_records.cite: Walker et al. Functional responses of methanogenic archaea to syntrophic growth. ISME J (2012). DOI:10.1038/ismej.2012.60. [PMC3475374]
growth_records.snippet: For coculture experiments, a culture medium flow rate of 1.3 ml min-1 (25.6 h retention time) was initiated when the OD600 reached 0.325-0.350... average concentrations measured during the steady-state growth experiments (4 mM lactate, 26 m
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady state so mu = D. D = 1/retention_time = 1/25.6 h = 0.0391 h-1 (also = flow/volume = 1.3 ml/min * 60 = 78 ml/h / 2000 ml = 0.039 h-1). Doubling time = ln2/mu = 0.693/0.0391 = 17.7 h. Substrates/products given only as steady-state reservoir concentrations (mM) or headspace partial pressures (Pa); no biomass dry weight reported, so specific rates (mmol/gDW/h) cannot be derived. Lactate consumed = feed 30 mM - residual 4 mM = 26 mM. CCM full composition not given in this paper (cited to Walker et al. 2009), so medium_composition_stated=false. Temperature 310 K = 37 C.

growth_records.strain: Dv Hildenborough / Mm S2; minimal assemblage from 1000-generation laboratory-evolved obligate-syntrophy line
growth_records.medium: Lactate coculture medium (sulfate-free, CCMA-based), with Resazurin anaerobic indicator

up.method: not reported as absolute specific rate; sustained lactate oxidation to acetate confirmed (Fig 1D); FBA model iSI1283 predicts relative lactate uptake flux per phase but no absolute mmol/gDW/h given in text

sec.method: measured qualitatively (organic-acid HPLC of supernatant); lactate oxidized to acetate by Dv, no absolute rate reported
measured by GC; steady-state headspace-corrected concentration ~0.75 mM after 72 h; headspace vented via one-way valve so value underestimates total produced; not a biomass-specific rate
electron-exchange intermediate Dv->Mm; production stoichiometrically proportional to lactate utilization (model); no absolute rate reported

product_yields.method: FBA model iSI1283 prediction (94% accuracy vs Stolyar et al. core model), NOT direct experimental measurement

growth_records.cite: Valenzuela et al. Origin of biogeographically distinct ecotypes during laboratory evolution. Nature Communications (2024). DOI:10.1038/s41467-024-51759-y. [PMC11358416]
growth_records.snippet: Biomass in the planktonic phase increased at a steady rate with a maximum growth rate of 0.23 d-1 and an average doubling time of ~3 days (Fig. 1B).
growth_records.notes: Growth rate reported by authors via R 'growthrates' logistic model. Reported mu = 0.23 d-1; convert to per-hour: 0.23/24 = 0.00958 h-1 ~= 0.0096 h-1. Cross-check with reported doubling time ~3 d = 72 h: mu = ln2/72 h = 0.693/72 = 0.00963 h-1 (consistent). Dilution rate 0.022 h-1 stated in Table 1 (7.8 mL/h feed / ~350 mL = 0.0223 h-1). Note: planktonic doubling (~3 d) exceeds reactor dilution (~3.7 d) so this ecotype is retained. Temperature ~30 C (temperature-controlled room); pH ~7.2. Medium = 'lactate coculture medium (Supplementary Data 1)' sulfate-free; full composition only in supplement, not main text, so composition:[] and stated:false (pre-culture/reference cocultures used CCMA medium + 30 mM sodium DL-lactate; monocultures 5 mL CCMA + 10 mM Na-acetate + 30 psi H2). Yields (H2/lactate 1.8, CH4/lactate 0.4 mol/mol) are FBA model predictions, flagged as such. No absolute qS/qP in mmol/gDW/h given in text (only relative fluxes mMol/gDCW/h in Fig 4).

growth_records.strain: Dv Hildenborough / Mm S2; minimal assemblage from 1000-generation laboratory-evolved obligate-syntrophy line
growth_records.medium: Lactate coculture medium (sulfate-free, CCMA-based), with Resazurin anaerobic indicator

up.method: attached Dv showed greater/more-efficient lactate utilization (FBA: higher relative lactate-uptake flux in attached phase, p=0.034); no absolute specific rate reported

sec.method: lactate oxidized to acetate by attached Dv; no absolute rate reported
H2 production stoichiometrically proportional to lactate utilization; attached Mm only partially utilizes H2 (low-affinity hydrogenases), excess released; no absolute rate
lower H2->methane conversion efficiency in attached Mm per FBA; no phase-resolved absolute concentration in text

growth_records.cite: Valenzuela et al. Origin of biogeographically distinct ecotypes during laboratory evolution. Nature Communications (2024). DOI:10.1038/s41467-024-51759-y. [PMC11358416]
growth_records.snippet: Using total protein from washed sediment as a proxy for biomass we determined that the attached community accumulated at a slower rate of 0.087 d-1 (doubling time: ~8 days) (Fig. 1C).
growth_records.notes: Attached-phase biomass accumulation rate reported = 0.087 d-1 (via total sediment protein as biomass proxy, R 'growthrates' logistic model). Convert to per-hour: 0.087/24 = 0.003625 h-1 ~= 0.0036 h-1. Cross-check reported doubling ~8 d = 192 h: mu = ln2/192 = 0.693/192 = 0.00361 h-1 (consistent). This growth rate (~8 d doubling) is far below the reactor dilution rate 0.022 h-1 (~1.3 d), consistent with a sessile biofilm retained by attachment rather than washout. Same medium/temperature/pH as planktonic record. No absolute qS/qP reported; only relative fluxes (mMol/gDCW/h) from FBA model iSI1283.

growth_records.medium: B3A medium (in tri-culture chemostat)

up.units: mM consumed (of 8 mM supplied; 2 mM residual)

up.method: gas/HPLC; inferred from C.c. acetate:ethanol:H2 ratio

sec.units: mol per mol cellobiose (sulfate reduced to H2S)

mfa.units: mol sulfate per mol cellobiose (6.1 mM total)
mfa.note: reduced to hydrogen sulfide; electron-donor limited (2 mM sulfate residual)

growth_records.cite: Miller, Lance D et al. Establishment and metabolic analysis of a model microbial community for understanding trophic and electron accepting interactions of subsurface anaerobic environments. BMC Microbiology (2010). DOI:10.1186/1471-2180-10-149. [PMC2906461]
growth_records.snippet: D. vulgaris depleted 6.1 mM sulfate (out of the 8 mM supplied) from the medium by sulfate reduction... sulfate remained in the medium at a concentration of about 2 mM suggesting that D. vulgaris was not growth limited by the amount of sulfa
growth_records.notes: Minor sulfate-reducer in anaerobic tri-culture chemostat. mu=D=0.03/h; doubling=23.1 h. Sulfate consumed 6.1 mM (measured, barium assay): volumetric 0.03x6.1=0.183 mmol/L/h. 2.7 mol sulfate reduced per mol cellobiose -> H2S (Fig 5 model). Electron-donor (H2/ethanol from C.c.) limited; growth not sulfate-limited. Incomplete TCA cycle (no CO2).

growth_records.medium: Defined sulfate-reducer growth medium (lactate electron donor, sulfite electron acceptor); same medium for batch and chemostat

up.method: calculated from chemostat mass balance: qS = D*(Cin-Cout)/X

mfa.note: lactate fully consumed to below detection; acetate held steady at 7.9 mM; consistent with 2:1 lactate:acceptor SRB stoichiometry
net cell-specific sulfite reduction; J_uptake = net rate of sulfite reduction at steady state

product_yields.note: 7.9 mM acetate / 10 mM lactate consumed = 0.79 mol/mol

growth_records.cite: Bertran et al. Deconstructing the Dissimilatory Sulfate Reduction Pathway: Isotope Fractionation of a Mutant Unable of Growth on Sulfate. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.03110. [PMC6302107]
growth_records.snippet: lactate was the limiting substrate (10 mM) while sulfite was in excess (20 mM)... a mu_max of 3.6 day-1 was obtained... Chemostat experiments were conducted at 10% of mu_max... the resulting dilution rate D... The biomass stabilized at a ce
growth_records.notes: Chemostat, DvH delta-Qmo mutant. Reported: mu_max(batch)=3.6/day; chemostat at 10% mu_max -> mu=D=0.36/day=0.015/h; td=ln2/0.36=1.925 day=46.2 h. V=0.5 L; flow 0.18 L/day (D=0.18/0.5=0.36/day). Steady-state cell density 2.16e8 cells/mL=2.16e11 cells/L. Reactor(=outflow) concs: sulfite 16.5 mM, thiosulfate 0.5 mM, sulfide ~10 mM, acetate 7.9 mM, lactate <detection. Inflow: lactate 10 mM, sulfite 20 mM. Volumetric rate=D*(Cin-Cout): lactate 0.36*10=3.6; acetate 0.36*7.9=2.844; sulfite drawdown 0.36*3.5=1.26; sulfide 0.36*10=3.6; thiosulfate 0.36*0.5=0.18 (mmol/L/day). Cell-specific=volumetric/2.16e11 cells/L (fmol/cell/day): lactate 16.67, acetate 13.17, sulfite 5.83, sulfide 16.67, thiosulfate 0.83. NOTE sulfur imbalance: sulfide produced (~10 mM) exceeds reactor sulfite drawdown (3.5 mM) due to gas/liquid trap partitioning; sulfide production (16.7 fmol/cell/day) is the better proxy for net sulfite reduction than reactor sulfite drop (5.8). Temperature: chemostat setup states room temp 21C; Discussion later refers to 25C for the reaction (noted). pH 7.2+/-0.1. Full defined-medium composition (salts) is in Supplementary Material, not in XML; only substrate/acceptor concentrations given here. gDW-based rates not derivable (no biomass in gDW reported; only cell counts) so rates given per-cell and volumetric.

growth_records.medium: Same defined sulfite/lactate medium as chemostat
growth_records.cite: Bertran et al. Deconstructing the Dissimilatory Sulfate Reduction Pathway: Isotope Fractionation of a Mutant Unable of Growth on Sulfate. Frontiers in Microbiology (2018). DOI:10.3389/fmicb.2018.03110. [PMC6302107]
growth_records.snippet: The maximum specific growth rate (mu_max) of the strain was first determined in batch culture in the same growth medium as used for the chemostat... a mu_max of 3.6 day-1 was obtained for the delta-Qmo mutant.
growth_records.notes: Batch mu_max determination reported directly: mu_max=3.6/day=0.15/h; doubling time td=ln2/3.6 day=0.1925 day=4.62 h. Measured from OD600 (converted to cell counts) exponential phase (Crozat et al. 1991 method). No uptake/secretion rates measured in batch phase; those come from the chemostat steady state (separate record). Same lactate(10 mM)+sulfite(20 mM) medium.
